text: But he gave none of the wagons or oxen to the Kohathite division, since they were required to carry the sacred objects of the Tabernacle on their shoulders.

content:
Yesterday we saw the lampstand in detail; what it was, how it was built,
how the priests covered it with a blue cloth and sea-cow leather before the Kohathites carried it on their shoulders through the wilderness. Today we look at what every part of that lampstand was pointing to. Just as we found with the Ark and the Table of Showbread, every single feature of the lampstand was a portrait of the Messiah, drawn in gold and fire and oil, long before He came. Paul says in Colossians 2:17 that the tabernacle items are shadows and the reality is found in Christ. Let us now look at the lampstand and find the reality it was casting a shadow of.

The lampstand was not made of wood covered with gold like the Ark or the Table. It was made entirely of solid, pure gold from the very base to the tip of every branch. Not a single part of it was made of any other material. Every cup, every bud, every blossom, every branch, every part of the shaft; one continuous piece of hammered pure gold throughout. There was no wood inside, no cheaper metal beneath, no hidden base of an inferior material. What you saw was what it was, all the way through.

This speaks of the divine character of Christ in its fullness and completeness. The Table and the Ark had wood inside gold; humanity and divine character held together. But the lampstand was gold alone. The lampstand in its entirety points to the divine source of the light Christ brought. John 1:4 says: “In Him was life, and the life was the light of men.” The light He carried was not a human light dressed up with a divine coating. It was divine life itself, pure and unalloyed, burning in the world. Colossians 2:9 says that in Christ the whole fullness of what belongs to God dwells bodily. The lampstand in pure gold declares that the light of Christ comes from a source that is entirely, completely, and without mixture divine.

The lampstand was made from one talent of pure gold; approximately 34 kilograms, or 75 pounds of solid gold. This was the single most expensive object in the entire tabernacle. Nothing else cost as much. And every gram of that weight was beaten by hand into its final shape. The craftsman did not pour it into a mold and let it set. He hammered it; striking, shaping, pressing, forming; until from a mass of gold there emerged the exact shape God had shown Moses on the mountain.

The weight speaks of the costliness of Christ to the Father. He was not sent cheaply. He was not given lightly. John 3:16 says God gave His only Son; the word “gave” carries the weight of a costly sacrifice. Isaiah 53:10 says it pleased Yahweh to crush Him. What pleased Yahweh was not the suffering itself but the salvation it produced; but the cost was real. The weight of the lampstand is the weight of what it cost to send the light into the world. And the hammering speaks of the suffering of Christ. He was beaten. He was struck. He was pressed and formed through suffering into the exact shape the Father had designed. Hebrews 2:10 says it was fitting for God to make the pioneer of salvation perfect through suffering. The hammer blows shaped the lampstand. Suffering shaped the Saviour.

The lampstand stood on a solid base; also hammered gold, also one piece with the entire lampstand. The base was what gave the lampstand its stability. It was what kept the whole structure upright through the daily tending of the priests, through the carrying of the Kohathites, through every move of camp across forty years of wilderness. Without the base, the lampstand would not stand. Without a firm base, seven lamps in the middle of the Holy Place would have been useless.

The base speaks of Christ as the eternal foundation on which everything else stands. 1 Corinthians 3:11 says: “No one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ.” Isaiah 28:16 says: “Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a tested stone, a precious cornerstone, a sure foundation.”
The seven lamps burn only because they rest on a base that does not move. The light of the congregation, the witness of the church, the testimony of every believer stands only because it rests on Christ as its base. Remove the base and everything falls. Rest on the base and nothing can knock it over.

From the base rose the central shaft; the main stem of the lampstand from which all six branches extended. The shaft was the source of every branch.
The branches did not grow from each other or from the base directly. They all grew from the shaft. Without the shaft, there were no branches. Without the shaft, there were no lamps. Without the shaft, there was no light at all in the Holy Place. The shaft was the most essential part of the entire lampstand.

This is one of the most precise pictures of Christ in all of the tabernacle.
In John 15:5 He said: “I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.” Christ is the central shaft. The congregation is the branches.
Every believer draws life, position, and light directly from Him. The branches do not hold the shaft; the shaft holds the branches. We do not sustain Christ. He sustains us. And just as all six branches of the lampstand came from one shaft and together produced one united light, all believers come from one Christ and together produce one testimony of light to the world.

We have now seen the gold, the weight, the base, and the central shaft; and already the lampstand is preaching Christ with remarkable precision. But we have not yet reached the branches, the three-stage almond flower on each one, the wick, the oil, the fire, and the light that burned in the Holy Place. Each of these has its own message, and together they complete the portrait. We will study these tomorrow. For now, let this settle: the lampstand was never meant to be just furniture. It was a picture of the one who would one day say, “I am the light of the world.”

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh, Your word is a lamp to our feet and a light to our path (Psalm 119:105).
You did not leave Your people in darkness. You placed a lampstand of pure gold in the middle of Your house and filled it with burning light, and You did this so that one day we would understand who Your Son was. We thank You for the costliness of the light that it was not sent cheaply, that every blow of the hammer that formed the lampstand speaks of the suffering that formed the Saviour. We thank You that He is our base; that we are not standing on our own strength or our own wisdom, but on a foundation that cannot be moved. And we thank You that He is our shaft; that we are not producing light by our own energy, but drawing everything from His life. Help us to abide in the shaft today. Help us to stay connected to the one from whom all light flows.
In Christ’s name, amen.
